business.title: An energy-aware and lightweight spiking neural network framework for real-time intrusion detection in IoT networks
business.desc: The rapid growth of Internet of Things (IoT) network has led to significant security threats due to increased rate of resource use and sophistication of cyberattacks. Traditional deep learning (DL) Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) utilize large amounts of resources, including energy and, therefore, cannot be implemented on low-powered IoT devices. In this paper, we present an Energy-Aware Lightweight IDS using Spiking Neural Networks (SNN-IDS) to provide high accuracy with respect to intrusion detection while maintaining low energy and memory consumption. This framework uses a hybrid-feature selection mechanism combining Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) and Mutual Information (MI) to reduce dimensionality and computation costs. In addition, we use SNN for the classification of intrusion events, which allows us to provide low-power, event-driven processing of the input to our system. We evaluate our framework on Bot-IoT, ToN-IoT, and CIC-IoT2023 security datasets, and the results indicate that SNN-IDS outperforms benchmark deep learning models, including CNN-LSTM and Transformer-CNN, in terms of detection performance, energy efficiency, and inference speed. When evaluated on the Bot-IoT dataset, SNN-IDS produces an accuracy of 99.12%, precision of 98.94%, recall of 99.05%, and an F1 score of 98.99%, while on the ToN-IoT dataset it produced 98.76% accuracy and an F1 score of 98.41% and on the CIC-IoT2023 dataset it produced 98.21% accuracy and an F1 score of 97.95%. The SNN-IDS also reduces energy consumption by 42.8% and inference latency by 35.6% when compared to CNN-LSTM based IDS, while maintaining comparable performance in terms of its ability to detect intrusions. Furthermore, the SNN-IDS reduces memory consumption by 38.3%, which makes it a suitable solution for deployment on edge-based IoT devices.

world.title: Stress, Screens And Sleep: A Brain Health Check
world.desc: Our brains are constantly adapting to the way we live. Every night of poor sleep, every stressful workday, every hour spent scrolling on a smartphone, and every long stretch of sitting can influence how well our brain functions. While occasional late nights or binge-watching sessions are unlikely to cause lasting harm, these habits can add up over time and affect memory, concentration, mood, and even long-term brain health.In India, where digital device use has increased rapidly and many people spend long hours at desks or on mobile phones, understanding how lifestyle choices affect the brain has become more important than ever.Stress: A Constant Burden on the BrainStress is a normal response that helps us deal with challenges. However, when stress becomes chronic, it can have harmful effects on the brain.Persistent stress keeps levels of cortisol, the body's primary stress hormone, elevated. Over time, excessive cortisol can interfere with the functioning of the hippocampus, a brain region responsible for learning and memory. Chronic stress has also been linked with difficulty concentrating, impaired decision-making, anxiety, depression, and reduced cognitive performance. In India's fast-paced urban environment, work pressure, financial responsibilities, long commutes, academic competition, and caregiving responsibilities often contribute to sustained stress levels. Managing stress is therefore not just important for mental well-being but also for preserving cognitive health.Why Sleep Is Essential for Brain HealthSleep is not simply a period of rest, it is when the brain performs essential maintenance. During sleep, memories are consolidated, toxins produced during daily brain activity are cleared, and neural connections are strengthened.Regularly sleeping too little or having poor-quality sleep can affect attention, reaction time, memory, emotional regulation, and problem-solving abilities. Over the long term, inadequate sleep has also been associated with an increased risk of cognitive decline and several neurological disorders. Unfortunately, insufficient sleep is becoming increasingly common. Late-night work, prolonged smartphone use, and irregular schedules often reduce sleep duration. Studies from India have also reported an association between increased smartphone screen time and sleep problems among adolescents and young adults, highlighting a growing public health concern. Screen Time: Not All Digital Use Is EqualDigital devices have become indispensable for work, education, communication, and entertainment. However, excessive recreational screen time may come at a cost.Research suggests that prolonged screen exposure may contribute to reduced attention span, mental fatigue, poor sleep quality, and increased stress. Blue light emitted from screens can delay the release of melatonin, the hormone that regulates sleep, making it harder to fall asleep when devices are used late into the evening. Importantly, experts note that the type of screen use matters. Activities that actively engage the brain, such as learning, reading, or solving problems, are likely to have different effects than hours of passive scrolling through social media or continuously watching short-form videos. Simply counting screen hours does not provide the complete picture. The Hidden Risks of Sitting Too MuchMany Indians now spend most of their day sitting, whether at office desks, in classrooms, commuting, or relaxing in front of screens. This sedentary lifestyle affects not only heart and metabolic health but may also influence brain health.Emerging research suggests prolonged sedentary behaviour may be associated with poorer cognitive performance and changes in brain structure, particularly when it involves passive activities such as prolonged television viewing. In contrast, cognitively stimulating seated activities like reading may not carry the same level of risk. The evidence is still evolving, but researchers consistently recommend reducing uninterrupted sitting time. Small Lifestyle Changes Can Protect the BrainBrain health is shaped by the combined effect of our daily habits rather than one single factor. Improving even one area often benefits the others. Better sleep can lower stress, regular physical activity can improve sleep quality and mood, and reducing screen time before bedtime can support healthier sleep.Simple habits can make a meaningful difference:•    Aim for 7–9 hours of quality sleep each night. •    Take short movement breaks every 30–60 minutes during prolonged sitting. •    Engage in at least 150 minutes of moderate physical activity each week. •    Avoid using smartphones, tablets, or laptops for at least an hour before bedtime whenever possible. •    Practise stress-management techniques such as yoga, meditation, deep breathing, or mindfulness. •    Spend time outdoors and maintain regular social interactions to support emotional and cognitive well-being. The Bottom LineThe brain is remarkably resilient, but it is also shaped by everyday choices. Chronic stress, inadequate sleep, excessive recreational screen time, and prolonged sedentary behaviour can work together to affect memory, attention, mood, and overall cognitive function. While research continues to explore exactly how these lifestyle factors influence long-term brain health, current evidence strongly supports adopting healthier daily routines.For Indians navigating increasingly digital and demanding lifestyles, protecting brain health does not require drastic changes. Consistent sleep, regular physical activity, mindful technology use, and effective stress management are practical, evidence-based steps that can help keep the brain healthy throughout life.

world.title: Relationship with Nicotine Concentrations Monitored by Molecular Fluorescence
world.desc: Cigarette butts are among the most abundant urban residues worldwide and constitute an emerging source of aquatic pollution due to the release of nicotine and other toxic compounds into the environment. In the present work, the acute toxicity of nicotine solutions and cigarette butt leachates was evaluated using fish, specifically juveniles of Poecilia reticulata, under controlled and standardized laboratory conditions. Acute toxicity assays of nicotine were performed, exposing fish to increasing concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 8.0 mg∙L−1 for 96 h. Mortality increased with nicotine concentration, obtaining an LC50 value of 2.0 mg∙L−1 for the studied species. Additionally, cigarette butt leachates were prepared under standardized laboratory conditions and tested at different dilution percentages (in a range of 2.5% to 20 %). The highest leachate concentrations produced elevated mortality and behavioral alterations, including severe mobility impairment in exposed organisms. Nicotine concentrations in the tested solutions were determined by a previously developed molecular fluorescence methodology based on the use of a luminescent metal-organic framework sensor. The analytical results demonstrated that nicotine concentrations in cigarette butt leachates were significantly lower than the LC50 experimentally determined for pure nicotine, suggesting that the observed toxic effects are associated not only with nicotine but also with the combined action of additional toxic substances released from cigarette residues. The present study highlights the ecotoxicological relevance of cigarette butt leachates as emerging aquatic pollutants and demonstrates the usefulness of combining biological models with fluorescence-based analytical methodologies for environmental monitoring applications.
